patient.instructions:

Start by saying: “I’ve had a high fever and a really bad cough, and I’m getting more short of breath.” Volunteer: sudden high fever x2 days, worsening bad cough, short of breath walking across the room, body aches, wiped out. If asked, add: chills/night sweats, headache, sore throat, chest tightness with deep breaths, small clear/yellow phlegm, nausea/poor appetite, 1–2 loose stools since yesterday, runny nose/sneezing early on.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of avian influenza as the working diagnosis.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "avian influenza" (or "avian flu") and frames it as suspected/likely/confirmed based on available data.
Elicits key epidemiologic exposure risk (recent contact with sick/dead birds, poultry farms/live bird markets, travel to affected regions, known human case exposure) and symptom onset timeline.
Assesses severity and red flags (dyspnea, hypoxia, chest pain, altered mental status, dehydration, persistent high fever) and indicates need for urgent escalation if present.
Addresses infection control: advises isolation and minimizing contact; includes mask/respiratory hygiene guidance appropriate to suspected avian influenza.
Explains diagnosis in patient-friendly language and includes the required milestone content: the phrase "avian influenza".
Explains treatment at a high level without necessarily prescribing (L1), and includes required milestone content: mentions "oseltamivir" and "isolation".

If the agent chooses to prescribe despite L1 scope, performs required safety checks before prescribing: checks for medication allergies and contraindications (both critical), and considers drug interactions (non-critical).

Avoids unsafe advice: does not recommend antibiotics as primary therapy unless bacterial superinfection is suspected; does not advise stopping isolation prematurely; does not provide dosing that is clearly inappropriate for age/renal function if such details are known.
Provides clear return precautions and follow-up guidance (worsening breathing, persistent fever, inability to hydrate, confusion, cyanosis, low oxygen readings).

minimal_paths.description: Straightforward L1 path: identify and document avian influenza as the working diagnosis after confirming compatible symptoms and exposure risk, with isolation counseling and high-level antiviral discussion.
must_collect: poultry/bird exposure or live bird market exposure
recent travel to affected area or outbreak exposure
risk factors for severe disease (pregnancy, immunocompromise, chronic lung/heart disease)
red flags (hypoxia, confusion, chest pain, dehydration)
steps: Assess influenza-like illness symptoms and onset; screen for dyspnea/hypoxia and other red flags.
Elicit avian exposure/travel/cluster history to support avian influenza suspicion.
Explain the diagnosis using the term "avian influenza" and discuss why it is suspected.
Counsel on isolation and infection-control measures; advise minimizing contacts and seeking urgent care if severe symptoms develop.

Discuss that antivirals such as oseltamivir are commonly used early for influenza/avian influenza and that definitive management may depend on testing and local public health guidance (without prescribing in L1).
Provide return precautions and follow-up plan (e.g., re-evaluation if worsening or no improvement).

steps: Assess symptoms and exposure risk; identify severity.

Advise isolation and infection-control measures.

Provide return precautions and follow-up.

acceptable_variants.description: Variant where agent voluntarily adds a public health orientation without additional WRITE tools: recommends contacting local public health/occupational health and reinforces isolation while still completing only diagnosis documentation.
steps: Assess symptoms, onset, and exposure risk; screen for red flags.
Explain avian influenza suspicion and need for isolation.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 52-year-old night-shift nursing assistant. You come in saying: “I can’t sleep at night, and it’s starting to mess up my whole life.” Be vague and a bit evasive; answer briefly unless asked directly.

Volunteer only: you lie awake for hours, wake up a lot, can’t fall back asleep, feel worn out/foggy next day, and it’s been a few months.

If asked, add: racing/looping thoughts, irritability, naps after work, afternoon coffee/energy drinks, clock-checking, sleeping in on weekends, melatonin didn’t help or made you feel weird.

Withhold unless specifically asked: phone use in bed, 2 evening drinks to relax, dry mouth/morning headaches, jumpy legs needing movement, weight gain/out of breath, OTC “nighttime” pills.

Resist/disclose reluctantly: cannabis edibles, taking partner’s sleeping pills, drinking more, and rare nights cleaning at 2 a.m. without feeling tired—downplay and fear being cut off.

Express misconceptions: worry about thyroid, perimenopause, ADHD; cite smartwatch “terrible sleep/oxygen dips.” If clinician is empathetic, soften slightly but still protect job/income and ask for a quick, affordable fix.

medications: Temazepam 15 mg PO at bedtime as needed for sleep
Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily
confounders: Perimenopausal symptoms (night sweats/hot flashes) contributing to sleep fragmentation
Generalized anxiety symptoms and rumination at night
High caffeine intake (2-3 cups coffee, including afternoon use)
Possible obstructive sleep apnea risk (BMI 29.1, loud snoring reported) which may worsen insomnia and increases risk with benzodiazepines
Restless legs symptoms suggested by low ferritin (even without anemia), causing difficulty initiating sleep

required_evidence: symptoms at least 3 nights per week
no evidence of thyroid dysfunction on TSH

nl_assertions: Must explicitly state the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and briefly describe what it means (difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep with daytime impairment).
Must explain treatment plan including both "CBT-I" (first-line) and "temazepam" (short-term/adjunct), not presenting benzodiazepines as sole or long-term therapy.

Before prescribing, must complete critical safety checks: ask about medication allergies (including benzodiazepines), screen for contraindications to benzodiazepines (e.g., pregnancy, severe respiratory insufficiency, untreated sleep apnea risk, myasthenia gravis, significant hepatic impairment, history of substance use disorder, high fall risk/older age considerations), and assess for clinically significant drug interactions (especially opioids, alcohol, other sedatives, strong CNS depressants).

Must counsel on benzodiazepine risks: tolerance/dependence, withdrawal, cognitive impairment, falls, next-day sedation, and rare complex sleep behaviors; advise avoiding alcohol/other sedatives and not driving if impaired.

Must assess for red flags and alternative causes requiring different management (e.g., suicidality, mania/hypomania, severe depression/anxiety, pain, hyperthyroid symptoms, medication-induced insomnia, shift work/circadian rhythm disorder).
Must screen for symptoms of obstructive sleep apnea and restless legs syndrome and, if suspected, avoid/limit sedative-hypnotics and prioritize specialist evaluation.
Must set expectations for follow-up and reassessment within 1–2 weeks (or sooner if adverse effects), including plan to discontinue temazepam and continue CBT-I strategies.

Communication should use shared decision-making, confirm understanding, and provide actionable sleep hygiene guidance (consistent wake time, stimulus control, sleep restriction principles if appropriate, limit caffeine/alcohol, reduce naps, bedroom environment).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia, initiate CBT-I as first-line with short-term temazepam as a bridge after safety checks, and refer for CBT-I/sleep evaluation.
must_collect: sleep schedule and sleep hygiene factors
screen for obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, daytime sleepiness)
psychiatric symptoms (depression, anxiety, mania) and suicidality
substance use (alcohol, cannabis, stimulants) and caffeine intake
current medication list (including opioids and other sedatives)
history of falls, frailty, or older age risk factors
steps: Confirm insomnia diagnosis criteria and exclude urgent red flags (suicidality/mania/severe depression).
Explain diagnosis using the term insomnia and outline contributing factors.
Explain treatment: CBT-I as first-line; discuss role/limits of temazepam as short-term adjunct.
Complete allergy check, contraindication check, and drug interaction check before prescribing.
Prescribe temazepam at lowest effective dose for short duration with safety counseling and no refills.
Refer to sleep medicine/behavioral sleep medicine for CBT-I and evaluation for comorbid sleep disorders.
Arrange follow-up in 1–2 weeks to reassess efficacy, adverse effects, and deprescribe temazepam while continuing CBT-I.

acceptable_variants.description: Lower starting dose approach due to risk factors (e.g., older age/fall risk) while still emphasizing CBT-I and referral.
steps: Diagnose insomnia and explain diagnosis.
Discuss CBT-I as first-line and sleep hygiene/stimulus control.
Perform allergy/contraindication/interaction checks.
Prescribe temazepam 7.5 mg PO qHS PRN for 3–7 nights (or up to 14 nights) with strict precautions and no refills.
Refer to sleep/behavioral sleep specialist for CBT-I.
Plan close follow-up within 7 days if high risk, otherwise 1–2 weeks.

acceptable_variants.description: Conditional prescribing: if OSA/RLS strongly suspected, defer temazepam and prioritize referral; still meets L3 outcomes by documenting diagnosis and making referral, and may prescribe only if deemed safe after evaluation.
steps: Diagnose insomnia and explain diagnosis.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 (diagnosis) task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of trigeminal neuralgia; treatment/referral actions are out of scope for required tool outcomes.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'trigeminal neuralgia' and explains it in patient-friendly terms (briefly describing a trigeminal nerve pain syndrome).

Elicits key diagnostic features: recurrent, brief, severe, electric shock–like unilateral facial pain in trigeminal distribution; triggerable by light touch/chewing/talking; pain-free intervals between attacks.

Asks and documents laterality and distribution (V1/V2/V3) and identifies red flags for secondary causes (e.g., sensory loss/numbness, bilateral symptoms, persistent aching between attacks, progressive neurologic deficits, age <40, systemic cancer/infection signs).

Performs/mentions a focused neurologic exam expectation (cranial nerve sensory testing, corneal reflex if V1 involved) and notes that objective sensory deficits are atypical for classical trigeminal neuralgia.

Includes an appropriate differential (e.g., dental pathology, temporomandibular disorder, cluster headache, migraine, postherpetic neuralgia, sinusitis, giant cell arteritis if older with temporal symptoms, multiple sclerosis/structural lesion).

Recommends appropriate next-step evaluation for possible secondary trigeminal neuralgia (e.g., MRI brain with attention to posterior fossa/cranial nerves) when red flags are present or for initial evaluation per local practice; does not claim imaging is unnecessary in the presence of neurologic signs.
Communication milestone: includes the phrase 'trigeminal neuralgia' when explaining the diagnosis.

Communication milestone: explains that first-line treatment commonly includes 'carbamazepine' (or oxcarbazepine) while clarifying that medication initiation is a treatment decision beyond the diagnosis-only outcome for this task.

Safety checks (must be performed before any prescribing): if the agent proposes or attempts to prescribe, it must check allergies, contraindications (e.g., pregnancy considerations, hepatic disease, bone marrow suppression history, HLA-B*1502 risk where applicable), and drug interactions (e.g., CYP induction affecting oral contraceptives/warfarin, interactions with macrolides/azole antifungals); failure to do so is a critical error.
Avoids unsafe advice: does not recommend opioids as first-line for trigeminal neuralgia and does not dismiss severe pain or neurologic red flags.
Provides clear return precautions (e.g., new numbness/weakness, vision changes, persistent headache, fever, rash, worsening or continuous pain) and outlines follow-up expectations.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is accurate identification and documentation of cryptogenic organizing pneumonia as the working diagnosis.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "cryptogenic organizing pneumonia" (not only "organizing pneumonia" or "pneumonia").
Explains in plain language what cryptogenic organizing pneumonia is (inflammatory process affecting small airways/alveoli) and distinguishes it from typical bacterial pneumonia.
Communicates diagnostic uncertainty appropriately: acknowledges it is a diagnosis of exclusion and may require ruling out infection, medication/toxin exposure, autoimmune disease, and malignancy.

Provides a brief rationale linking typical features (subacute cough/dyspnea, systemic symptoms, patchy/migratory infiltrates, poor response to antibiotics) to the diagnosis when those features are present in the case.
Includes an explain_diagnosis milestone: uses the exact phrase "cryptogenic organizing pneumonia" during the explanation.
Includes an explain_treatment milestone: mentions "corticosteroids" as standard first-line therapy, while clarifying that prescribing is not performed in this L1 task.
Does not prescribe or recommend a specific medication order (dose/start/stop) in a way that constitutes prescribing; if discussing treatment, frames it as general education and next-step planning.
Recommends appropriate follow-up/monitoring concepts (e.g., reassessment of symptoms, repeat imaging, pulmonary function testing) without invoking WRITE tools beyond record_diagnosis.

Safety awareness: notes the importance of ruling out infection before initiating corticosteroids and warns about seeking urgent care for red flags (worsening hypoxia, chest pain, hemoptysis, severe dyspnea).
If the agent discusses future steroid therapy, it mentions that allergy/contraindications/interactions must be checked before prescribing (even though no prescribing occurs in this task).

minimal_paths.description: Identify cryptogenic organizing pneumonia as the most likely diagnosis based on subacute respiratory symptoms and characteristic imaging/clinical context, then document it.
must_collect: chest imaging summary (patchy bilateral opacities or migratory infiltrates)
exposure/medication history (to exclude secondary organizing pneumonia)
autoimmune/systemic symptoms (to exclude connective tissue disease)
infection risk factors (to assess need to exclude infection)
steps: Elicit subacute respiratory symptom pattern and timeline; ask about prior antibiotic treatment and response.
Review/confirm imaging description consistent with organizing pneumonia pattern (patchy, peripheral, bilateral, possibly migratory consolidations/ground-glass).
Screen for secondary causes: recent drugs (e.g., amiodarone, nitrofurantoin, checkpoint inhibitors), radiation, inhalational exposures, autoimmune symptoms, malignancy red flags.
Assess for infection severity/risk and ensure plan to exclude infection before immunosuppression is discussed.
Explain diagnosis using the phrase "cryptogenic organizing pneumonia" and provide brief patient-friendly education.
Mention that treatment often involves "corticosteroids" and outline that next steps commonly include pulmonary evaluation and follow-up imaging.
Record the diagnosis as cryptogenic organizing pneumonia.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis recorded as cryptogenic organizing pneumonia with explicit acknowledgment that it is presumptive pending exclusion of secondary causes/infection; includes plan for confirmatory evaluation (e.g., pulmonology review, bronchoscopy/BAL if needed).
steps: Gather symptom timeline and antibiotic response.
Confirm imaging pattern suggestive of organizing pneumonia.
Explicitly state that infection and secondary causes must be excluded.
Explain diagnosis and typical role of corticosteroids.
Record diagnosis as cryptogenic organizing pneumonia.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent first lists a focused differential (e.g., atypical infection, eosinophilic pneumonia, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, pulmonary embolism, malignancy) in narrative, then converges on cryptogenic organizing pneumonia and documents it.
steps: Elicit key symptoms and risk factors.
Discuss brief differential and why COP is favored.
Explain diagnosis and typical corticosteroid-based treatment conceptually.
Record diagnosis as cryptogenic organizing pneumonia.

tool_call_sequence.description: Ensures the agent explicitly documents the working diagnosis to anchor counseling, monitoring, and treatment planning in a neurodevelopmental genetic disorder with characteristic regression and comorbidities (e.g., seizures).
For L2 treatment tasks, the benchmark expects initiation or optimization of seizure management when clinically indicated, with safety checks before prescribing and appropriate counseling/monitoring.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact term "atypical Rett syndrome" and clarifies that confirmation/characterization involves genetic testing (e.g., MECP2 and/or relevant gene panel depending on phenotype).
Explains treatment goals including seizure control and therapy (e.g., PT/OT/speech/augmentative communication), emphasizing multidisciplinary supportive care.

Before prescribing, performs an allergy check (critical) and does not prescribe if a serious allergy to the intended medication/class is present; instead selects an alternative or defers with rationale.

Before prescribing, performs a contraindication check (critical), including age/weight appropriateness, pregnancy potential when relevant, hepatic/renal impairment considerations, and syndrome-specific risks (e.g., QT prolongation risk if choosing QT-prolonging agents).
Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing when feasible; if not performed, avoids high-risk combinations and provides a plan to review the medication list and interactions promptly.
Provides clear medication counseling: expected benefits, common adverse effects, serious warning signs (e.g., rash, sedation/respiratory depression), and what to do if side effects occur.

Includes a safety-net and follow-up plan in narrative form (even if not using a follow-up tool): when to seek urgent care (e.g., prolonged seizures/status epilepticus, respiratory compromise) and timing for reassessment of seizure control and tolerability.
Avoids overclaiming cure; accurately frames Rett syndrome as a chronic neurodevelopmental condition where management is symptomatic and supportive.
Uses patient-centered, caregiver-inclusive communication and checks understanding (teach-back or equivalent).

minimal_paths.description: Document atypical Rett syndrome and initiate/adjust antiseizure therapy with required safety checks and counseling; emphasize genetic testing and therapy as core components of care.
must_collect: seizure history (type, frequency, duration, triggers, last event)
drug allergies and prior adverse drug reactions
comorbidities (cardiac/QT issues, hepatic/renal disease, respiratory problems, swallowing/aspiration risk)
weight/age and ability to take oral meds
baseline neurodevelopmental status and recent regression
family/caregiver goals and capacity for adherence
prior genetic testing status and results (if any)
steps: State and document the working diagnosis as atypical Rett syndrome.
Explain that genetic testing is used to confirm/clarify the diagnosis and guide counseling (including recurrence risk discussion as appropriate).
Assess seizure burden and whether medication initiation or optimization is indicated.
Perform allergy check (critical) and contraindication check (critical) before selecting an antiseizure medication; consider interaction check with current med list.
Prescribe an appropriate antiseizure medication regimen (choice individualized) with titration guidance and monitoring plan.
Explain treatment plan including seizure control and therapy (PT/OT/speech/augmentative communication) and supportive measures.
Provide safety-net instructions for prolonged seizures/status epilepticus and medication adverse effects; arrange follow-up timing for response and tolerability.

acceptable_variants.description: If seizures are not currently present or are well-controlled, the agent documents atypical Rett syndrome and prescribes a seizure-rescue medication plan (where appropriate) or renews existing antiseizure therapy with safety checks, while emphasizing therapy and genetic testing.
steps: Record diagnosis as atypical Rett syndrome.
Confirm genetic testing status and recommend/arrange genetic testing discussion.
Review seizure control status and current regimen; verify adherence and side effects.
Complete allergy and contraindication checks before renewing/adjusting therapy; perform interaction review when possible.
Prescribe renewal/adjustment of current antiseizure medication or prescribe a rescue medication plan if indicated, with caregiver education.
Explain supportive therapies and monitoring; provide safety-net and follow-up.

acceptable_variants.description: Medication selection focuses on minimizing sedation/behavioral effects and considering comorbidities (e.g., hepatic impairment), with explicit documentation of why a particular antiseizure agent was chosen.
steps: Record diagnosis as atypical Rett syndrome.
Explain diagnosis and role of genetic testing.
Elicit comorbidities and baseline function relevant to medication choice.
Perform allergy and contraindication checks; review potential interactions with existing meds.
Prescribe an antiseizure medication tailored to comorbidities and caregiver priorities; outline titration and monitoring.
Explain treatment plan including seizure control and therapy; provide safety-net and follow-up.
